skill.name: giil
skill.description: Download images from public cloud photo share links for remote AI and terminal workflows. Supports iCloud, Dropbox, Google Photos, and Google Drive links with browser-based capture options.
skill.summary: Downloads public cloud photo share images for AI-assisted debugging and review.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, and filesystem patterns in SKILL.md. Most are documentation examples, but the remote curl-to-bash installer pattern is a confirmed high-risk publication issue for a community skill. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious payload was found in the reviewed file.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly To Shell
high_findings.description: The documentation recommends downloading an installer from a remote GitHub URL and piping it directly into bash. This can execute unreviewed remote code on the user system and is a real supply-chain risk, even though no malicious payload is present in this file.
high_findings.recommendation: Replace pipe-to-shell installation with a signed release download, checksum verification, and instructions that let users inspect the installer before execution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky installation pattern is explicitly documented in two sections. The file does not contain the installer body, so this confirms execution risk but not malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Browser Automation Against User Supplied URLs
medium_findings.description: The skill describes loading public cloud share links in headless Chromium, intercepting image responses, and taking screenshots. This is core functionality, but it exposes users to untrusted web content and privacy-sensitive image capture flows.
medium_findings.recommendation: Document sandboxing expectations, avoid authenticated browsing contexts, and warn users to run only against public share links they trust.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The described capture strategy clearly uses browser automation and network interception. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it remains security-sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Local Cache Writes And Destructive Cleanup Commands
medium_findings.description: The documentation describes installing files under user-local cache and binary directories, then removing cache directories during uninstall. The removal commands are scoped, but they are destructive filesystem operations users may paste directly.
medium_findings.recommendation: Provide safer uninstall guidance with path checks and avoid recommending recursive deletion without context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are scoped to giil and Playwright cache locations, which reduces impact. The recursive deletion commands are still real destructive filesystem operations.

medium_findings.title: Image Data Can Be Piped To External Services
medium_findings.description: The base64 workflow shows image output being posted to an external API. This can be useful, but it may expose private screenshots or photos if users copy the command without reviewing the destination.
medium_findings.recommendation: Add privacy warnings for base64 and upload workflows, and recommend reviewing destinations before transmitting image data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is an example using a placeholder API endpoint, not hidden exfiltration. The privacy risk is still real because the workflow transmits image bytes.

low_findings.title: Sudo Package Installation Examples Are User Visible
low_findings.description: The documentation includes sudo package manager commands for optional HEIC support. These are expected dependency instructions, not stealth privilege escalation.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep the commands documented as optional prerequisites and state that users should review package names before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo usage is visible documentation for installing named system packages. There is no evidence of hidden elevation or malicious command construction.

low_findings.title: Scanner False Positives Without Supporting Context
low_findings.description: Several static findings, including weak cryptography, Windows SAM, and path traversal, appear to be triggered by unrelated words, environment variable names, or abbreviated shell examples. No semantic evidence supports those threats in SKILL.md.
low_findings.recommendation: Do not treat these specific matches as blockers unless matching executable code or sensitive file access is added.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation text and shell placeholders at the cited locations. No Windows SAM access, weak cryptographic implementation, or path traversal behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation recommends downloading an installer from a remote GitHub URL and piping it directly into bash. This can execute unreviewed remote code on the user system and is a real supply-chain risk, even though no malicious payload is present in this file.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Replace pipe-to-shell installation with a signed release download, checksum verification, and instructions that let users inspect the installer before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky installation pattern is explicitly documented in two sections. The file does not contain the installer body, so this confirms execution risk but not malicious intent.

content.user_title: Download Cloud Share Images for AI Workflows
content.value_statement: Remote AI work often needs screenshots or photos that live behind cloud share pages. This skill describes a CLI workflow for downloading public shared images into terminal sessions.
actual_capabilities: Downloads images from public iCloud, Dropbox, Google Photos, and Google Drive share links.
Uses browser automation strategies for JavaScript-heavy photo share pages.
Supports file path, JSON metadata, base64, and resolved URL output modes.
Can download multiple images from shared albums with indexed filenames.
Documents HEIC and HEIF conversion support using platform tools.
Provides debug artifacts such as screenshots, page HTML, and Playwright traces.
limitations: It requires public share links and does not bypass authentication.
First use may download a large Chromium browser dependency.
Cloud provider page changes can break selectors or capture behavior.
The reviewed package contains documentation only, not the installer or CLI source.

use_cases.title: Remote UI Debugging
use_cases.description: Download a phone screenshot from a public cloud share into an SSH session for AI-assisted debugging.
use_cases.target_user: Developers using Claude Code or Codex on remote machines

use_cases.title: Design Review Capture
use_cases.description: Collect shared screenshots and photos from common cloud providers for review in a terminal workflow.

use_cases.title: Album Intake For Documentation
use_cases.description: Download public shared album images with stable filenames for issue reports, docs, or visual records.

prompt_templates.title: Download One Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a single public share link.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use giil to download this public image share link and tell me the saved file path: [paste link].

prompt_templates.title: Prepare For Visual Debugging
prompt_templates.scenario: Use an image in a remote AI coding session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Download this screenshot with giil, then inspect it for UI layout issues in the current project: [paste link].

prompt_templates.title: Capture Album Images
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for public shared albums.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use giil album mode on this public album link and summarize the downloaded files for review: [paste link].

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Capture Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Run giil with debug and verbose output for this link, then explain why capture failed and what to try next: [paste link].

output_examples.input: A developer shares an iCloud screenshot link during a remote debugging session.
output_examples.output: The assistant downloads the image, reports the local path, and uses it as visual context for the bug investigation.

output_examples.input: A QA reviewer provides a public Google Photos album with several mobile screenshots.
output: Images are saved with ordered filenames.
The reviewer receives a concise list of usable files.

output_examples.input: A cloud share link fails because it requires login.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that the link is not public and asks for a public share link or an uploaded file.

best_practices: Use only public share links that you are authorized to access.
Review installer commands before running them, especially remote scripts.
Use debug output when provider pages change or captures are incomplete.
anti_patterns: Do not paste private or authenticated cloud links into shared terminals.
Do not pipe downloaded images to external APIs without reviewing the destination.
Do not run recursive cleanup commands unless you understand the target path.

faq.question: Does this skill bypass private cloud photo permissions?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for public share links and reports authentication errors when access is required.

faq.question: Which cloud services are described?
faq.answer: The documentation covers iCloud, Dropbox, Google Photos, and Google Drive image share links.

faq.question: Why does it use Playwright?
faq.answer: Some cloud share pages rely on JavaScript and dynamic image loading, so browser automation can capture the real image.

faq.question: Can it download full albums?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented album mode downloads multiple public shared photos with indexed filenames.

faq.question: Is the reviewed skill safe to publish as is?
faq.answer: No. The report marks it high risk because it recommends piping a remote installer directly into bash.

faq.question: Does the reviewed package include the CLI source code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file structure contains only SKILL.md, so the installer and runtime code were not available here.
